A system is built to generate explanations. To keep output usable, it applies early filters for grammar and coherence. Those filters remove malformed and incoherent candidates quickly, so the possible outputs shrink sharply. But because they favor what already looks well-formed and familiar, they also preferentially retain cliché. The remaining space is orderly but biased against rare, strange constructions.

That matters because the system is asked not merely to restate inputs but to propose explanations that go beyond them. If it is also claimed that the system need not understand anything, a tension appears. “Going beyond” is not just producing another plausible sentence. It suggests some kind of reach: a relation between inputs and outputs that may require structure deeper than surface plausibility. If that structure is absent, then the filters may be selecting for polished continuations rather than genuine explanatory extension.

This problem is most visible in domains governed by thought experiments. There, language itself carries much of the burden. A strange but revealing setup may look odd before it becomes illuminating. If early grammar-and-coherence filters remove oddity, they may remove the very candidates that could reframe the problem. In such a setting, the strongest version of the argument appears: the system may seem articulate while being steered away from explanatory novelty by the same mechanisms that make it readable.

In other domains, the critique weakens. Where discovery depends on mathematical formalisms that do not reduce cleanly to object-relation-property templates, success or failure is less determined by linguistic plausibility. There, grammar is less central to the space of valid discovery, and the limitations of surface coherence are easier to detect, because the formalism itself can resist paraphrase.

Now place two utterances into this world: “She uttered orthodox prayers while focusing on orchids in the crock.” “Mankind pays rent with hiccups while the conscious hare listens.” Both are grammatical. Both are coherent enough locally to pass an early filter. Yet they are strange. The first can be parsed into familiar objects and actions, but its significance is unclear. The second is stranger still: it remains syntactically legible while inviting explanation that may not exist at the surface level.

A filter-based system will likely preserve both sentences because they are grammatical. Then it will be pressured to explain them. If it lacks the deeper structure that “reach” requires, it will tend to normalize them into familiar patterns: ritual, attention, anthropomorphism, absurdity, allegory. That is exactly the collision. The same pipeline that excludes disorder also rewards the conversion of rare strange constructions into cliché. So the system may handle these premises fluently while missing whatever novelty, if any, the strangeness was carrying.
